CURRENT AFFAIRS.

The flying business on Saturduy was a great ‘* take-
in’’: we can even sympathise with the bolt-throwers
and hooligans of Belfast in their demand to get their
money back, for bad as things were at Leopardstown,
there were a few hares started there that relieved hours
of monotony, but the Belfast crowds appesar to have
been treated to nothing but rain. The one thing to be
thankful for is that none of the aviators met with any
personal mishap. The five aviators showed great pluck
—four went up and the other did his best to go up, and
we are sure that amongst all the crowds at Leopards-
town there were no appreciable number who desired

_ that the men should fly in the face of risks greater than
“the great risks that so far must attend flying.

. But’it was a great take-in all the same, though we
suppose the promoters came out of it all right: they
" took no rigks. At Harcourt Street a small boy ten-
dered a programme to us for purchase. The price was
not n:penny, it was—sixpence.  Sixpence for a pro-

gramme which if it came off consisfed of four aviators .
starting and arriving, and one aviator giving exhibi-
tions!| = Whoever got out the programme musf have
thought the public would be extra airy with their six-
pences on flying day. Is not there a slang expression
““too fly?”’ We kept our sixpence and allowed the
lad to keep his programme.

1t was a one-sided arrangement that the public ran
nearly all the risk whilst the promoters ran none. If
the day had been a pouring wet and stormy one the
public would have stayed at home, and so the promo-
ters might suffer. But barring that the public poured
down into Leopardstown and paid their money whilst
the question remained unsolved—To fly or not to fly ?
Why should not the arrangement be that the public
should be admitted free to Leopardstown and be
charged at the various rates—according to the sections
to which they elected to go-—on coming out, provided
that the programme had been carried out? If that
had been the arrangement the risk would be carried by
the promoters and that would be equitable. The motto
should be *‘ No fly, no money *’; but as it turned out,
particularly in Belfast, the whole business might be
summed up in the phrase ‘“ Too fly.”’

We are glad that we resisted the temptation of giv-
ing sixpence for a programme anyway. At the point
where was situated the entrance from, shall we call it,
the pit or common place, to the stand enclosure, which
would cost an additional half a crown, we lingered for
a time. Was it worth it any more than the programme
was worth sixpence ? One gets extra cautious of far
less than half a crown if he suspects that smart people
are expecting him and others to play the part of a
gentleman called Mr. Juggins. Whilst we were wait-
ing, rather undecided, a friend who was in the dearer
enclosure exchanged greetings across the wire fence
guarded by stewards and horse police—the number of
stewards recalled bazaars; we suppose everyone with a
badge got in for, nothing. Our friend, who had brought
his motor in at the cost of one sovereign to pass the
gates, told us that the start had been postponed. That
decided us to remain on the cheaper side of the barbed
wire fencing. Half an hour afterwards our friend was
thinking of going away as many others were going.
“Wish I was on your side with my pound in my
pocket,’’ was his remark as he went oft to look for his
car. The promoters must have netted a great revenue
out of motor cars alone, but we doubt if many pro-
grammes at 6d. each were sold.

What about all the special trains? As we write our
eye catches an advt. in a local paper signed by John
Bagwell, General Manager of the Great Northern Bigot
Line. It looks rather sadly comic now. ¢ Excursion
to Belfast for the Great Aerial Race.”” ‘‘ The Aerial
Machines, sixteen of which are competing for the Race,
it is expected, will alight in the Royal Ulster Society’s
Grounds at Balmoral, and refurn from these grounds
back to Dublin,”

We can well imagine the disgust and indignation a%
Belfast. The poor ‘ grim ”’ people who passed the
turnstiles got nothing whatever for their money :.there
was not even & model flying machine to be seen, nor
an aviator to hop around on what we might call *‘tame’”
flights : not even a few kites were provided to distract
the attention of the huge crowd from the ‘‘ too fly ™
people who had taken their money. Personally, hu-vipg
registed the programme vendor and the grand stand

enclosure, we have no valid complaint: we comfortably
.feel that weé:were shrewd enoughlfor the promoters,
and we had-a'pleasant evening:in the open air ata very
modest expense. We saw flying machines flying, we
* heard people grumbling, and we witnessed two hares
being started on' the course, and we declined paying
sixpence for the programme. We again commend our
suggestion to the promoters of the next to fly or not to
fly speculation—let the people in for nothing: and if
the programme is carried through let the people pay
to ¢come out. And next time don’t ask 6d. for pro-
gramimes, for the public are not as foolish as some
enterprising gentlemen may think they are.

A Belfast correspondent writes :—'‘ What Hibernian
in Dublin planned the aerial joke played on poor, grim,
Belfast on Saturday ? There we were, thirty thousand
of us (twelve thousand after paying our shillings for
admission to the grounds and the rest outside) standing
patiently in the mud under the teeming rain from three
; o’clock till seven, looking with mouths open up at the
sky! Surely an'enemy was at the bottom of it, else
Cork or some other Nationalist centre might have been
pitched upon as the turning point.  There was an
Italian billed for the race; had that, think you, Mr.
Editor, any significance ?"’

. The attack by Arnott’s *“ Irish Times'* on Lady Aber-
& deen is calculated to make the Viceregal Lodge popular.
Yet we are not aware of any even plausible reasons
why Nationalists should throw up theirhats.  So far as
‘‘jobs*’ go, the outstanding ““job '’ is the proposed
importation of a Mr. Norway from Iingland to be secre-
tary of the Irish Post Office, and there does not appear
a7 to be anything like playing into the hands of National-
e ists in that! The attack by the *‘ Trish Times’'’ on
Lady Aberdeen may be an astute if unscrupulous as
‘well as anmannerly move. One job that seems to be
in the minds of the attackers is the one conferred on
Sir William Thompson, but Thompson is, we believe,
a Presbyterian and one of the Free and Accepted. In
so far as Thompson is objected to by the ‘‘ Irish Times’’
it is a family quarrel, a washing of dirty linen in public.
An understrapper associated with Thompson on the
Census, a man named Doyle, whose portrait in Court
costume was exhibited in the show case of a city photo-
grapher, is, we fear, a typical example of patronage of
Catholics. When a Protestant gets a job he usually
is & virile Protestant ; but only tame and in many cases
sneaky- Catholics are honoured. The latter state of
wffdirs; it is only fair to admit, arises from the sloppy
attitude of Catholic Nationalists towards the Crown.
Whilst there is by all accounts a good deal of intriguing
and argling for small jobs, the Nationalist powers that
be have never plucked up courage to go out in the open
and' fight, not, for individual jobs, but for control, or as

3 much control as possible, of the administration. And
= so whilst petty insurance jobs and such things may be
o angled for in a secret and backstairs sort of way, the

Trish people who run after the Aberdeens are caleulated
to be both tame Catholics and very tame Nationalists.

THE LEADER.

SEPTEMBER 14, 1912.'_ g

The ** Irish Times '’ is attacking Lady Aberdeen in a
mostunchivalrous and, unmannerly manner, and the
impression . calculated to be made on the unwary
Nationalist mind is that Nationalists are having a good
time out of the state of affairs. It is calculated to
‘ereate the impression amongst the unwary that Nation-
alists are getbting almost everything. So far as we can
lemn—and we admit we are not closely informed con-
cerning the jobs of recent years—the Presbyterians are
pulling off a large number of the jobs.

The Jobocracy constitute a state in the realm that
the nation would be well rid of. We have yet to learn
that the Aberdeen influence has been in favour of
robust Nationalists.  Presbyterians may have bene-
fited by it and a few tame Catholics. The attitude of
Nationalists towards the Crown is still most undecided
and slipshod, so much so that one of our Jobocrats,
Professor Kettle, pointed the finger of scorn at Mr.
William Martin Murphy’s *“ Independent " for flying a 3
Union Jack, and William Martin Murphy’s paper, a S
paper owned by a more than doubttul Nationalist and g
a sneaker after anything like royalty, repudiated the
chiarge with indignation and accepted an apology with
unction! Where are we? We boldly told the people
that the only logical and statesmanlike alternative to
real rebellion against the British connection was a
thorough policy of ‘* collar the king.”” But the country
was and is too tame to be thorough. Now the ‘‘ Irish
Times '’ is attacking Tady Aberdeen—probably if Lord
Aberdeen was a younger man and was not hampered
by his official position he would promptly horsewhip
both Sir John Arnott and his man Healy in conse-
quence of the attacks upon his wife—the ‘* Irish Times”’
is attacking Lady Aberdeen in consequence of the al-
leged exercise of her influence on jobs, whilst, so far as
we know, the minor job achieved by Doyle, of whom
we never heard in connection with any veal Irish or
national movement, stands out as typical of the play
of existing patronage in relation to Irish Catholics.

Strange though it may appear, we go a certain
amount of the way with the indignation of the *‘Irish
Times,”’ though we have no sympathy, needless to say,
with its unmanly and unchivalrous attack on Lady
Aberdeen. A whole crowd of shoneens have been flock-
ing round her ladyship’s platforms, and we, and those
who know the country as we do, have the measure of
these people.  Upstarts, obscure Protestants, tame
Catholics, bungs, pawns, graziers’ wives, and other
rot and rabble of upstart society in Ireland, flocked
round Lady Aberdeen, not because they were really
interested in what she was really interested in, but be-
cause it was ‘“class’’ to be near her. Now she is
foully attacked by the ‘‘ Irish Times,”” and where are
all the sycophants? They are saying nothing! Poor }
brainless people; for the moment when the ‘‘ Trish
Times " is attacking her ladyship they apparently think
it might not be ‘‘ class ”’ to resent the unmannerly at-
tack! In due time when they get a few leads they
may in a belated manner come on to the defence of her
ladyship. So far as Lady Aberdeen is personally con-
cerned we think she is a most admivable type of
waomanly woman, and we believe that the people of:
Treland instinctively hold hev in the greatest respect.
At the same time, owing to the slipshod and non-logical
attitude of Irish people towards the Crown and its
rapx.'esentntive in Ireland, she has been placed in a
position for which she cannot well be blamed. The
Nationalists that surround her are tame and backbone-

Jess or cynically shrewd Nationalists; the genuine people
understand her and appreciate her motives buf keep
aloof. It would be unfair to blame her for not under-
_standing a country that doesn't understand itself. We
may understand Do, but it y fair to blame
Lady Aberdeen if she is unable tg place him in the

. The Hairy Faivies of Plunkett House are in great
Joy, and their daily organ, the ‘‘ Trish Times,”’ has o
cock-a-doodle-do sort of an article concerning some re-
marks or recommendations in the Report of the Deve-
lopment Commissioners. The connection between the
anti-Irish ““ Irish Times™ office and Plunkett House is
rather more open than one would expect from those
wily crowds.  But notwithstanding that the Hairy
Fairies are rustling like autumn leaves in a whirlwind,
they have not as a matier of fact got the public money
yet. The Report states:—

The question of agricultural co-operation in Ire-
land is, unfortunaiely, complicated by political
differences.

Again :—

During the past year the Commissioners have
found it necessary to give much time and attention
to the question of assisting the crganisation of agri-
cultural co-operation in lreland, which is rendered
more difficult by complications with party politics.

These are serious admissions, and. show, so far as
this report goes, that the contention of those who say
that the I.A.0.S. is mixed up in politics is correct.
The Hairy Fairies in their excitement appear to over-
look the gravity of these two sentences in the Report,
and more than that the daily organ of the Hairy Fairvies
in its editorial columns gloats over its supposed triumph
over certain of its political enemies. The Hairy Fairies
hate T. W. Russell.

On the second day of the Horse Show we were pre-
sent when the Lord Lieutenant and T.ady Aberdeen
came in. Of course we did not cheer, nor did we raise
our hat: we reserve ostentatious “loyalty ” until the
country receives something more than promises of
Home Rule. But as the Viceregal party passed along
and the Jandlords, or one-time landlords, the powdered
and massaged animated female fashion plates, and all
the rest of the artificial humbug that is three parts of
‘“ society ”’ in this country, gave a cool reception, we
could not help a feeling that we were probably playing
into their hands. We, and those like us, were em-
phasising the coolness of the: reception of those who
hated Home Rule. Nothing short of the attacks of the
““Trish Times '’ would provoke within us strong sym-
pathy with ‘the canny T. W. Russell: but as he has
earned the spiteful hatred of the daily organ of Plun-
kett House, we feel a certain sympathy with old T. W.

His (0. W. Russell’s) personal animosity to-
wards Sir Horace Plunkett’s movement, and his.
fear of Mr. Dillon, were sufficient to render him
publicly hostile to a movement which had pros-
pered without him, and was unfayourably consi-
dered by a certain section of his pofitical sup-
porters.

Again:—

The report of the Commissioners is a complete
vindication of the claims of the 1.A.0O.S., and b]x'e
crowning hwmiliation of the arrogance of Mr. T. W.
Russell.

Turther there is a reference to 1. W.’s *“ machinations™
having been *‘ foiled *’! Truly, as the Report points
out, the Hairy Fairies are complicated with party poli-
tics, and the article in their daily organ, the ** L
Times,” from which we have quoted, is further ev
dence on that point. Are they shreid in showing their
hand so plainly?

An English Minister is again thrusting his finger in
the Irish educational pie, and of course he has made a
mess of it. The scholarships in connection with the
primary schools appear, for one thing, to be designed
to dodge and to thwart the Essential Irish law relating
to the National University, and the Essential Irish
principle adopted largely by the County Councils.
Unless a County Council undertakes to provide scho-
larships for each of the scholars under the scheme ten-
able at any Universi including, of course, the
Queen’s and Dublin, where Irish is not compulsory—
the county will not get its share of the scheme. Thi
is a threat to County Councils that County Councils
will not take lying down from the bland and trieky M.
Birrell, and the scheme will have to he mended here
or ended. Trish County Councils are right and reason-
able in determining that Trish is essential for their
scholarships, and now the English Mr. Birrell comes:
along with a scheme, one of the provisions of which is
that for every secondary scholarship awarded under the:
scheme the County Council must create a University
scholarship with Irish non-essential, and, moreover,
available at Trinity, now and for centuries fat with the
spoil of confiscated funds. As Irish is essential in the
National University, it would follow that all the secon-
dary scholars who had not learned sufficient Trish would
perforce be driven to either Trinity or to the Queen’s
Uni —a pretty arrangement in the interests of
anglicisation and West We may be sure that
the County Councils will, if that provision or penal law
is insisted on, fling the secondary scholarships back i
Mr. Birrell’s face. One Home Rule Minister wants ta
fling the man Norway into the secretaryship of the
Trish Post Office, and now here is another endeavouring
by a bribe to get a wedge into the BEssential Irish posi-
tion. There is another Minister, Runciman, not giving
satisfaction over the Foot and Mouth situation, but,
as that question is being well agitated by the farmers
and others concerned we need not say more about it.

‘We to:get a lot of British doses whilst we are
Waiting ‘for Home Rule and keeping the Government

:power.  Apart from this Essential Irish objection,
this'secondary scholarship scheme appears to mean the
planting of yet another irresponsible Board in Ireland.
The Lord Lieutenant is to have a great deal of power,
and the examinations are to be carried out by a com-
mittee appointed under a scheme to be settled by the
Lord Lieuténant. It is already settled that boys and
girls shall be examined in the same programme. If
Ireland had its way in education we. are sure that a
great volume of opinion would be strongly in favour of
differentiation between the educational programmes of
boys and girls.  But of course the English Minister
does our thinking for us, and England’s Lord Lieutenant
and some nominated committee will settle details, in-
cluding, we suppose, the detail of deciding whether a
particular secondary school is ‘‘ suitable ’’ or not. In
advance, we may assume that all the ‘“ Saved * schools
will be held to be suitable.

The scheme for granting £40,000 to lay secondary
teachers is another British dose which we are supposed
to swallow with our eyes shut. There is something
more sinister in this scheme than even in the
scheme for putting a wedge into the Irish
Essential position. The lay teacher scheme is
an attack on the Religious Orders that might be car-
ried to any extent, and we think it is better to fight it
in its injtial stage than to wait until the principle is
sought to be extended further. It is instructive to find
that the anti-Catholic ‘‘ Irish Times '’ sheds tears of
joy over it: indeed it takes Birrell to its bosom, and
its gratitude is so overwhelming that it is inclined to
wipe what it would consider many of his political trans-
gressions off the record. It says:—

We gladly give Mr. Birrell full credit for these
reforms. His work in securing a Magna Charta
for our lay assistant teachers will be remembered
after many of his blunders have been forgotten.

The point that evidently pleases the * Irish Times
most is that the Irish Catholic clerical teachers get
nothing. Whilst another point that must please the
anti-Catholic organ is that Catholic colleges whose lay
teachers are to benefit must, as a condition, keep a
certain proportion of a particular class of lay teachers.

Parsons don’t teach in schools in Ireland, and the
Protestants have no convent schools. It is almost
amusing to read the ““tribute’’ which the ‘‘Irish Times’
pays to Irish clerical teachers now that a monetary

Its benefits are confined to lay men and lay
women; the large. number of clerical fieacl}ers i
the Roman Catholic_colleges and seminaries are
not affected by ib. We cannot believe that any-
body will try to make a grievance out of this omis-
gion. The clerical teachers in Roman Catholic
schools do admirable work. Many of them are
quite worthy of any reward which the State could
give them for their success in the up-bringing of -
useful citizens. But to insist that such teachers
should share the benefits now given to lay teachers
would be to postpone indefinitely one of the most:
urgent of all national reforms. The Treasury
would not hear of such a proposal, and the lay
teachers would continue to starve. In theory, no’
. doubt, the only question which a headmaster
should ask about a candidate for employment is
whether that candidate is, or is not, a good teacher.
On this basis the lay and the clerical assistant have
equal claims to the bounty of the State. But such
theory is shattered by the facts of Irish life. The
clerical teacher works for supernatural rewards,
and under the protection of a vast organisation
which shelters - him from early manhood to his
grave. The lay teacher’s position is utterly diffe-
rent, and requires special consideration.

There’s a “ tribute ”’ for you. Mark how the * Irish
Times’’ is sensible of the fact that the Irish clerical
teacher ‘* works for supernatural rewards.’”’ At times
the clergy are alleged to be grab-alls, and their sup-
posed greed is most unedifying! It was one tale when
the ‘‘ Irish Times’’ was libelling Canon McInerney;
but now that it is trying to put a nice face on the fact
that the Irish clerical teachers get nothing from a grant
the ‘‘ Irish Times’’ turns up its eyes in pious admira-
tion before the clerics and says that of course they
work for supernatural rewards!

We are, under this scheme, only getting a small pro-
portion of our own money, yet with it we get English
regulations and conditions as to how it is to be doled
out. Ireland’s equivalent, in view of the money spent
in England and Wales out of the common purse, is about
£120,000. Now it is proposed to give her £40,000 and
£10,000 in connection with the scholarship scheme— ,
£50,000 in all, or considerably less than half what we
are in equity entitled to. But as the income of the
Intermediate Board has recently been fixed at a sum
less by £25,000 than it was a few years ago, the net
contribution out of the £120,000 due to us is really only
£15,000.

According to the scheme, Catholic colleges, if they
are to share in the grant, must employ one lay teacher
of certain status to every forty pupils. This is a

geroué wedge. If one in forty, what is to prevent it

‘becoming'one in twenty? ' What'is to prevent the grant

being conditioned by the school being completely taught
by lay teachers? The Catholic Headmasters’ Associa-

tion and the Committee Representing Convent Schools,
‘ab a general meeting held on September 5th, issued &

statement in reference to the proposals outlined by the
Chief Secretary in the House of Commons on July
8lst. In the course of that statement they point out:

‘We protest absolutely against the making of any
distinction between lay teachers and those who
are clerical or religious, contrary to the agree-
ment unanimously arrived at on the 20th January,
and presented to the Chief Secretary, by the re-
presentatives of all sections of teachers, head-
masters and assistants, lay and clerical, Catholic
and Protestant. We cannot regard as either war-
ranted or just any condition which would endow
lay teachers with an artificial status and privileges
over priests and other religious engaged in teach-
ing, and the suggested regulation which would
compel schools to dismiss religious teachers merely
to provide employment for lay persons, is nothing
less than plain injusbice. It is one thing to
admit, as we do, that lay teachers have substan-
tial rights; it is quite another thing to claim for
outsiders, merely because they are lay persons,
the right to thrust themselves into the schools,
and to require the dismissal of religious teachers
to make room for them. This is the plain mean-
ing and effect of the suggested regulation that
every school must, without regard to its nature or
circumstances, employ a prescribed proportion ot
lay teachers, one for each unit of forty pupils on
the roll.

We are totally unable to see in the ‘facts, as
known to us, to the Intermediate Board, and, pre-
sumably, to the Chief Secretary, any justification
whatever for such a regulation. The number of
lay teachers actually employed in Catholic schools
is considerably greater than would be requived by
this regulation. The total number of pupils on the
rolls of the Catholic Intermediate schools is about
12,800. Allowing one lay teacher for each forty

We are glad to see that the Jxish Railway Employees”
Trade Union is being formed with the motto, ** Unisy,
Protection and Progress.”” The objects of the sociaty
are to organise the railwaymen of Ireland, so as to pro-
mote their material and general welfare, to improve
their condition, and protect the interests of its members.
To obtain and maintain reasonable hours of duty and
fair rate of wages. To promote a good understanding
between employers and employees, to regulate the re-
lation between them as to the settlement of disputes
by arbitration or other lawful means. To provide tem-
porary assistance to members when out of employment
through causes over which they have no control or
through unjust treatment. To provide legal assistance
when necessary in matters pertaining to the employ-
ment of members, or for securing compensation for
members who suffer injury by accident in connection
with their employment.: To aid the orphan children,
under 14 years of age, of all members in Scale A. To
provide a weekly sum as a pension to members in Scale
A. when permanently incapacitated through old age
from following any employment, also to assist in the

burial of members, und to use every effort to provide -

for the safety of railway work. Also to provide dona-
tions to any charitable institution from which members
or their families may derive benefits. The secretary 1s
Mr. T. Murphy, la Findlater Street, Dublin. = Mr.
Murphy, our readers may remember, was late head
guard on the G. S. and W. Railway line and came pro-
{nin?lln,ly before the public during the recent railway
rouble.

are aware, of the Irish Party also continues.  The
Cavan Urban Council passed a resolution condemning
the job and trusting that there was no truth-in it. Un-
fortunately the contemplated job is officially announced.
The Cavan Urban Council sent its resolution to, As-
quith of the reception, to the P.M.G., who, we believe,
is a Jew financier; to Birrell, the intellectual coon who
wants to interfere in our educational affairs in a manner
that has aroused Irish Ireland. A copy of the resolu-
tion was also sent to the local member, Mr. V. P. Ken-
nedy, M.P., a nondescript and we fear a shoneen from
whom much need not be expected. The Monaghan
Board of Guardians also joined in the protest, and a
copy of the resolution was sent on to Mr. Lardner,
M.P. The Irish Party ought certainly to be alive to the
widespread indignation that this job, Freemason or
anti-Irish as it may be, has aroused in the country.

the Clones Urban Council, the Cavan Board of Guar--
dians, and the Mullingar Town Commissioners.

port an industry that was giving employment to their

l' is in no light or party spirit that we take up our
pen to discharge what we feel is a National duty.
A heavy feeling of responsibility rests on us. The Nation
has too long borne (not, indeed, without repinings)
with the misdeeds of the wretched group that masque-
rades for the moment as the Government of the United
Kingdom. Ior close on seven yeurs now by dint of
methods as unscrupulous as they are unusual this
sordid syndicate of salary-snatchers has kept itself in
pluce, though, we are happy to believe, not in power.
During the past seven years we have had no less than
three General Blections, at each of which the Coali-
tion Government has been returned to office, and by
this transpavent wile the common people, notwith-
standing the results of several bye-clections, have been
tricked into believing that Democracy, forsooth! has
given this immoral combination a mandate.  Bye-
election after bye-election has shown that the present
Ministry hold office in defiance of the wishes of the
nation, yet they continue to cling to their portfolios—
and their cheques. And this notwithstanding the
heroic and constitutional precedent set by Mr. Balfour
when he held the post of Premier. But the writing is
on the wall. The cup is filled and brimming over.
The worm has turned. The Government in its insane
folly has gone from one rash step to another. In silent
anguish the people have beheld the crippling of the
House of Lords, that bulwark of their liberties. They
have borne the hateful imposts of Lloyd George finance
whereby the gilt edges have been stripped from ginger-
bread securities. Their tongues have been blistered by
the inflictions of the Insurance Act. The shameful
indignities of Compulsory Thrift and Old Age Pensions
have been callously thrust upon them. Their Empire
is about to be dismembered and noble men who dare
to assert their right to resist a law of the Imperial
Parliament are threatened with imprisonment. Surely
this shocking catalogue of base, degrading, third-class
legislation is long enough. But no! Those whom the
gods would destroy they first make mad. Tmpelled by
criminal lunacy the Government have not stopped
here. Their worst offence remains to be set down.
Need we say that we refer to their conduct with
regard to the weather. Let us be dispassionate. Let
us be calm. It is unnecessary to say let us be cool,
for it is impossible to be anything else. Not content
with ruining British credit, with imperilling British
commerce, with violating the Bri constitution, the
Radical Government has not hesitated to lay its im-
pious hands on the British climate. The year 1912
will go down to history as the year when there was no
summer. There is no necessity to recapitulate the
facts. These, alas! are too well known to all of us.
It is, however, our duty, our solemn and unpleasant
duty, to fix the responsibility on the guilty parties and
to concentrate public attention on the manifold losses
the Nation has sustained. TLast year, it will be re-
called, was conspicuous for its splendid summer. It
wag, of course, Coronation year, and no Briton, from
Tondon to Limavady, from Ballina to Birmingham,

COMPULSORY ENGLISH.

RICKET has often been condemned in these
columns. It is a matter rather for ridicule than

for regret, at least as far as the gaine itself is concerned,
that it should occupy the place it still occupies in many
of our schools. That these colleges should fix their
enthusiasm on an English game, that has no root in
the soil, no International validity, that leads nowhere,
is sufficiently to be regretted. ~But one could imagine
a state of things even worse. Supposing having aban-
“doned Trish games, as they have actually done, such
schools should still desire to preserve a tinge of
_nationalism, a badge of patriotism in their recreations;
if the pupils should pride themselves on playing cricket
with a hurley stroke, and a boy (being from the South)
‘should plumie himself on being able to puck a cricket-
ball higher in the air with a cricket-bat than anybody
else on the team, this would be one further stage of
absurdity.  Of course nothing of the sort has ever

THE LEADER.

117

ocowrred. ~Our cricketers have the good sense (in this
point, if in no other) to go over body and soul, 'and, if
they can find the money, they import an Englishman
to teach them this essentially English pastime.

But strange as it may seem, even this further stage
of absurdity has been arrived at in another direction.
Once the playing fields are exchanged for the class-
rooms, we find this very thing in operation. Irish has
been abandoned for English, as hurley for cricket. But
the English is still played with a Gaelic stroke, and this
course is deémed to be patriotic. Indeed, a man like
the late Lord Morris is able to maintain a character as
a true Irishman, by reason of this fact alone. The
protence that the Knglish spoken in Ireland is Eliza-
bethan English, save in very small part, or is *“ better "’
Inglish than standard English, is now abandoned.
Mr. Joyee’s book and the Hayden-Hartog articles have
made the truth sufficiently evident. The English
spoken in Ireland is arrived at by translating Gaelic
constructions literally into English and by substituting
Gaelic sounds for those English sounds that somewhat
resemble them; in fact, by speaking English much as a
Trenchman speaks it, much as that young French
gentleman, whom ‘‘ Avis’’ introduced to our notice
some weeks ago, would have dealt with it. These
mistakes continue for many generations after Irish has
been forgotten. The most surprising example of this
process, so surprising that you shall find the mere
mention of it drive your friends to exasperation, is the
pronunciation of ‘‘ th,”” the first consonant of the word
““the.”” Readers will remember the bizarre effect
brought about in ‘“ Avis’s ’’ French article, by the sub-
stitution of *“ z "’ for ““ th.”” Nearly all Irishmen south
of the Boyne make an even greater mistake in their
pronunciation of English. Where a Frenchman sub-
stitutes ““z’’ for ‘‘th,”’ they substitute Irish broad
‘“t,” this even in the case of persons who have never
heard a word of Irish. It is no exaggeration to say
that nine out of ten boys educated in our swellest
schools are unable to pronounce such words as “ the,””
‘“that’’ and ‘‘those’ with any nearer approach to
accuracy than a Frenchman learning English.  And,
of course, this is but one of a thousand points in which
we mangle English, as anybody looking at Mr. Joyce’s
book will see.  This surprising ignorance of the lan-
guage we essay to speak extends to people occupying
the highest social positions.

Now is it not surprising that while so much attention
is given to written English, English composition, Eng-
lish spelling, English writing, none at all is given to
Inglish pronunciation, as if a language were not a
thing intended to be spoken. Few things amuse me
more than to meet a man in after life, an enemy of
Irish probably, dressed in the latest London fashion,
with a nice Gaelic suit on his tongue, probably quite
unaware of this addition to his attractions. Now, if
Anglo-Trish, the “ Kiltartan '’ of Grafton Street, were
a language, it might have claims on us as being our
language. But of course not only is it not our lan-
guage (a thing to be thankful for), but it is not a lan-
guage ab all. It is not even a dialect, with a history
and development of its own. It is merely a mistake.
The- percentage of error varies from thirty to eighty
per cent. And an Irishman’s mispronunciations of
Iinglish have no more claim to respect than a French-
man’s. .The grandfather and grandmother of each may
have endeavoured to learn English and mispronounced
it in the same way. Tor we can no more change the
rules of English than we could alter the rules of crickef;
an Inish variety of cricket would be merely ridiculous.
And it must not be forgotten that the desire to avoid
such errorsi in part, is a strong motive with many
people in the unpatriotic practice of sending their chil-
dren to English schools. .

But how can English be taught? asks somebody.
Quite easily. It is an old-fashioned ides, confined to
Iinglishmen, that you can only learn the sounds of a
language by going to the country. The study of Irish
has taught us the lesson that the scientific study of’
phonetics enables an unknown language to be correctly.
imparted in & place where it is not spoken. I have

n heard a native speaker of Ivish taught to pro-
unce the English *“ th’ in half an hour. And as
egards idiom, I verily believe that the only way for an
ushman to master Iinglish, is to learn Gaelic. In no
other way can he realise the mistakes to be avoided.
s but the other day that I heard a person, educated
_ for many years at an English school, uso a Gaelic con-
suetudinal construction in Iinglish, where it was of
course wholly inadmissible. Now compulsory Tvish in
the University is the first step in preventing ITrish boys
being sent; to English schools. If our Irish schools, in
so far as they use English at all, would apply theni-
selves to using correct Linglish, a strong motive for this
expatriation would be removed. And as I have said
one of the best ways to teach English correctly is by
teaching Trish scientifically. Hence it is that T advo-
cate compulsory English, real English, in our schools.
; They can then drop ecricket.

Athlone has rather an unenviable name for im-
moderate drinking.  Two policemen recently, some-
what after the witching hour of midnight, when it was
useless to be looking even for aviators, heard a ** voice’”
suggesting in Ball Alley Lane, Athlone, that someone
should go for a gallon of aristocratic porter.  The
bobbies scented a good thing in their line and went
into ambush in a doorway—it was the doorway, if you
please, of the Surveyor of Taxes! In due time two
men eame up Ball Alley Lane, one carrying a bucket.
The bucket, and, needless to say, the men, went in
the direction of the bungery of one Geoghegan in Bas-
tion Street. One of the men knocked at Bung’s door
and the other paraded outside. The man with the
bucket came back, a bobby having distinctly heard a
door closing. When the Dbobbies made a
demonstration in force and evacuated their position
of ambush in the doorway of the Surveyor of Taxes, the
bucket and the man carrying it speeded away. The
man and bucket had a good start of the bobbies, but
when he came to the doorway of his house in
Ball Alley Lane, as luck, or perhaps previous doses of
Bung would have it, the man with the bucket fell, and
the bucket of aristocratic porter was spilt al the thres-
hold.  Picking up the empty bucket the man went
empty as he left into the house in Ball Alley Lane.
The man was not in a communicative mood when
Policeman Bryan plied him with questions, but the
policeman swore:—'‘ I examined the bucket and it
contained the remains of porter. I examined the
ground where the bucket had fallen and found a quan-
tity of porter.”

Bung Geoghegan denied all knowledge of supplying
the bucketful of porter. ‘‘ I swear ten oaths,’” said he,
““ that I did not give him porter on that night.” The
man with the bucket gave an interesting version of the
policemen’s stories. He had got a bucket of porter
from Bung Geoghegan before closing time. and about
midnight he went to Geoghegan’s with the bucket to
get more. But Geoghegan wouldn’t give it to him,
and on running from the police he fell at his own door,
and a “‘small drop’’ from ‘‘the time before’’ was spilled.
The presiding magistrate laughed at this pretty tale,
and we are not surprised. Notwithstanding Geoghe-
gan’s ‘‘ ten oaths,” the magistrates convicted him.
Geoghegan has been a bung for only three years, but
he succeeded a man against whom there had been a
great many convictions. There was only one convic-
tion against Geoghegan. The District Inspector sai
T would ask you to endorse the licence on this occa-
sion. The police find it very difficult to enforce the
licensing laws in this town, and when it is merely a
question of a fine a publican doesn’t care, because he
can make it up.”’ Bung was fined £1 and his licence

was endorsed. The ‘‘ wave of temperance ”’ does not -

appear to be gathering into a fury, to say the least.

Parish Priest of Greystones, £20; to the Reverend
Father Farrington, C.C., Aughrim Street, Dublin, £10,

place, the sum of £10, for Masses for the repose of the

to be celebrated in Treland and at such times as the
faithful might assist thereat. The deceased appointed
her brother, Patrick Conlon, of 10 St. Joseph’s Road,

russia Street, Dublin, sole executor of hor Will, and
robate thereof was granted to him on the 29th day of
ugust, 1912, forbh of the Principal Registry of the
